metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete structured-assertion command to AC3 that extracts and checks the `overlap` key from the verdict output — e.g. `rebar review-plan <ticket> | python -c "import sys,json; v=json.load(sys.stdin); assert 'overlap' in v, v"`  (or equivalent `jq -e '.overlap' <verdict-file>`) — so the check cannot pass if the key is absent.
findings.finding: AC3's proving command for `overlap` key presence in the verdict is prose-level, not a structured assertion — it says `rebar review-plan <a ticket> returns a verdict carrying an overlap key` but supplies no command to extract and assert on the structured output, leaving the check unverifiable by defect-taxonomy type 6 (prose-vs-structured assertion target).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (`rebar review-plan <a ticket> returns a verdict carrying an overlap key`)
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the prose note with a runnable assertion:
```
rebar review-plan <any-ticket> --output-format json \
  | python -c "import sys, json; v=json.load(sys.stdin); assert 'overlap' in v, f'overlap key missing: {v}'"
```
If the CLI emits structured output to a file, use `jq -e 'has("overlap")' verdict.json` instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises path #2: perform at least one `rebar` ticket write (e.g., `rebar push` or append an event) after setting the flag, and verify the enrichment drain is invoked (e.g., via a log line, a metric, or a direct assertion on `enrich_drain.py` being called with `overlap_enabled=true`).
findings.finding: The enrichment drain (path #2) — which the plan explicitly flags as a 'real latency/cost side-effect on write paths' activated by this flag — has no acceptance criterion verifying it runs end-to-end. All ACs can be satisfied (TOML parses, config reads true, `review-plan` returns overlap key) while the drain wiring in `event_append.py`/`push.py` is silently broken. This is a defaulted-path with no live exercise.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (AC3) / 'Two consumers of the flag' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 or a new AC: 'After setting the flag, run `rebar push <ticket>` (or equivalent write path) and confirm the enrichment drain fires — e.g., `rebar --debug push <ticket> 2>&1 | grep -i overlap_drain` exits 0 or emits a drain-invocation log line, proving `event_append.py`/`push.py` wiring reaches the drain under `overlap_enabled=true`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly defer with rationale to a named child ticket) that verifies the enrichment drain path does not error when `overlap_enabled = true` — at minimum a smoke-test such as `rebar ticket write <test-ticket>` completing without drain-related errors, or a documented rationale for why drain correctness is out of scope here.
findings.finding: The enrichment drain (consumer 2 — `enrich_drain.py` activated on every ticket write) is enabled by this flag flip but has no acceptance criterion that exercises or even smoke-tests this code path, despite the plan acknowledging it introduces real latency/cost side-effects on write paths and has an LLM external boundary — making it a cutover with no end-to-end check.
findings.location: Two consumers of the flag — consumer 2 (enrichment drain); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers the drain path)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 (or as AC6): 'A `rebar` ticket-write operation (e.g. `rebar ticket update <any-ticket>`) completes without error and logs/emits no drain-related exception when `overlap_enabled = true`. If the drain lease/soak rules prevent firing in CI, confirm via unit test that `enrich_drain.py:maybe_drain()` is called (not skipped at the flag check) — e.g. `pytest tests/test_enrich_drain.py -k overlap_enabled`.  If drain correctness is intentionally deferred, add a comment naming the child ticket that covers it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) to verify the enrichment drain path executes at least once after the flag is enabled — e.g. confirm a drain invocation is logged or a digest record is written on a test ticket write.
findings.finding: The Two Consumers section describes the enrichment drain as a real, active behavioral side effect triggered on every ticket write, but no Acceptance Criterion covers verification that the drain fires (or is correctly bounded/gated) after the flag is enabled. The described work has no corresponding AC.
findings.location: Two Consumers section (enrichment drain description) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Scope's 'No source/behavior change' claim with the Two Consumers section's acknowledgment of real write-path latency/cost side effects — either qualify the Scope statement (e.g. 'no source change; behavior change is the intentional activation of two existing gated paths, including write-path enrichment') or remove the contradictory phrasing.
findings.finding: The Scope section states 'No source/behavior change' but the Two Consumers section explicitly acknowledges 'a real latency/cost side-effect on write paths' from the enrichment drain being activated. These two claims directly contradict each other and would send an implementer in two directions when deciding whether to audit write-path performance or communicate the change to contributors.
findings.location: Scope paragraph ('No source/behavior change') vs. Two Consumers section ('real latency/cost side-effect on write paths')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'No source/behavior change' with 'No source change; the only behavioral change is activating two existing gated code paths (see Two Consumers), including opportunistic write-path LLM enrichment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan: (a) the expected/acceptable latency budget for the plan-review overlap step (e.g., 'advisory, non-blocking, target <Xs'), and (b) whether `overlap_drain*` tunables need to be set (state their defaults and confirm the defaults are appropriate for this project before merging).
findings.finding: The plan activates LLM calls on every ticket write (opportunistic enrichment drain) and on every plan-review (retrieve-then-rerank overlap step) but states no latency budget or time-bounded done-definition for either path, and does not specify whether the `[tool.rebar.llm] overlap_drain*` tunables require explicit configuration to bound write-path latency before the flag is enabled.
findings.location: Section: 'Two consumers of the flag (behavioral note)' and AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope or AC section, add a note such as: 'Confirm `[tool.rebar.llm] overlap_drain_*` defaults are appropriate (e.g., `overlap_drain_max_per_run`, lease/soak intervals) — document the values in `rebar.toml` alongside the flag if non-defaults are needed. The plan-review overlap step is advisory and non-blocking; target end-to-end latency addition <Xs per review run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 'Two consumers' drain paragraph, name the operative default drain mode (`async` — detached subprocess) and note that the `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` fast-path gate (default 20 ms) keeps the common-case write overhead negligible, so contributors know what to expect without reading `LLMConfig` defaults.
findings.finding: The plan states the drain side-effect introduces 'real latency/cost side-effect on write paths' but does not disclose the operative default drain mode ('async' — detached subprocess per qualifying write), leaving contributors unaware that they will immediately start seeing background subprocess activity on every ticket write once digests exist in the queue, even before the overlap step surfaces useful results.
findings.location: Plan section 'Two consumers of the flag (behavioral note)' — drain side-effect paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the drain paragraph with: 'The default drain mode is `async` (a detached subprocess that outlives the command), gated by a cheap ≤20 ms queue-scan check; if nothing is soaked the gate exits immediately and no subprocess is spawned. Set `[llm] overlap_drain = "off"` in `rebar.toml` to disable subprocess spawning while keeping overlap detection on the review path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the 'Two consumers' behavioral note (bullet 2) naming the valid `overlap_drain` enum values (`off` / `async` / `always`) and their effects, so a contributor can tune or suppress the drain side-effect without reading source.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Two consumers' behavioral note names `[tool.rebar.llm] overlap_drain*` tunables as the control knob for write-path latency/cost side-effects, but never discloses the valid value vocabulary of the `overlap_drain` enum (`"off"`, `"async"`, `"always"`). A contributor reading only the plan to tune or suppress the drain side-effect is pointed at an underdefined knob — the processing protocol (which drain mode to set) is referenced without the decision vocabulary needed to act on it.
findings.location: Plan section 'Two consumers of the flag (behavioral note)', bullet 2 (Opportunistic enrichment drain)
findings.suggested_fix: In the bullet-2 drain description, append: 'The drain mode is controlled by `[tool.rebar.llm] overlap_drain` (valid values: `"off"` — disabled, `"async"` — detached child (default), `"always"` — inline/synchronous for CI). Setting `overlap_drain = "off"` suppresses all drain-triggered LLM calls without setting `overlap_enabled = false`; `overlap_enabled = false` (full rollback) disables both paths together.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and document in the plan) that the enrichment drain failure mode on write paths is fire-and-forget / fully async so that LLM unavailability or timeout never propagates to the ticket write caller.
findings.finding: The plan activates an enrichment drain on ticket write paths (event_append.py, push.py) that makes LLM calls, but does not specify what happens when the LLM dependency is slow or unavailable during a write — whether the write degrades gracefully, times out, or propagates an error to callers.
findings.location: ## Two consumers of the flag — 'Opportunistic enrichment drain' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a behavioral note: 'The drain is invoked asynchronously and its failure (timeout, LLM error) is swallowed/logged; the ticket write always completes independently of the drain result.' If this is not yet true in the code, it should be an explicit constraint on the implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that existing instrumentation in `enrich_drain.py` and the plan-review overlap step covers drain errors and step failures (or add a note to the plan if new logging/metrics are needed).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify any observability (metrics, logs, or alerts) for the two new failure points activated by the flag: enrichment drain errors and overlap-step LLM call failures.
findings.location: ## Two consumers of the flag (behavioral note)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document whether BM25F digests have a defined retention/eviction policy (TTL, deletion on ticket removal, or explicit 'accumulate-forever is acceptable'), and clarify the expected store state after rollback.
findings.finding: The plan does not address a retirement or cleanup path for the BM25F digests that the enrichment drain will begin accumulating in the store once the flag is enabled.
findings.location: ## Two consumers of the flag — 'Opportunistic enrichment drain' paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Scope section's 'No source/behavior change' claim with the confirmed write-path enrichment drain side-effect — either remove the contradictory phrase or qualify it (e.g. 'no source change; behavior change on write paths as described below').
findings.finding: The Scope section states 'No source/behavior change' but the plan's own 'Two consumers of the flag' section explicitly confirms a real behavioral change on write paths: every `rebar` ticket write may now opportunistically trigger LLM enrichment (latency/cost side-effect). This internal contradiction could mislead code reviewers or operators into approving the change under the impression it is purely cosmetic, when it is not.
findings.location: Scope section ('No source/behavior change') vs. 'Two consumers of the flag' behavioral note
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'No source/behavior change' in Scope with: 'No source change. Behavior change: write paths may now trigger opportunistic LLM enrichment (see Two consumers section); rollback is setting the flag to false.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC3 to specify that the `rebar review-plan` invocation must be a real (non-dry-run, sidecar-emitting) run, since the `overlap[]` key is gated on BOTH `emit_sidecar=True` AND `overlap_enabled=True` (see `plan_review/__init__.py:510,513`).
findings.finding: The AC's behavioral claim that 'rebar review-plan <a ticket> returns a verdict carrying an overlap key' is contingent not just on `overlap_enabled = true`, but ALSO on `emit_sidecar=True` at the call site. The source at `plan_review/__init__.py:510` shows `if emit_sidecar:` is the outer guard, with `overlap_enabled` checked inside. A pure-read invocation (emit_sidecar=False) will not produce the `overlap` key even with the flag enabled — the AC's verification step could silently pass or fail depending on invocation mode, and the plan never mentions this co-condition.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'rebar review-plan <a ticket> returns a verdict carrying an overlap key'
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC3 to read: '…`rebar review-plan <a ticket>` (a real sidecar-emitting run, not a dry-run/pure-read) returns a verdict carrying an `overlap` key…', and note that `emit_sidecar` must be True for the key to appear.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
